summary: An Indian minority religion (one of the Sramana movements, alongside Buddhism) centered on ahimsa, or strict nonviolence toward all living things. Followers venerate 24 tirthankaras ("ford-makers"), the most recent of whom, Mahavira, is traditionally regarded as the founder of the religion's current cosmic cycle in the 6th century BCE. Divided into two main sects, Digambara ("sky-clad," naked monks) and Svetambara ("white-clad"), whose largest schism partly concerns whether the 19th tirthankara, Mallinatha, was male or female and whether women can attain moksha. Distinctive doctrines include anekantavada ("many-sidedness") and its sevenfold syadvada logic, and practices include sallekhana (ritual fasting to death) and daily rites like samayika and pratikraman.

works.name: Mahavira
works.description: The 24th and final tirthankara of the current cosmic half-cycle, traditionally regarded as the founder of Jainism as practiced today, dated to the 6th century BCE. Son of King Siddhartha of the Ikshvaku dynasty's Jnatr (Gyat) clan; some traditions hold he was a prince of Kosala. Svetambaras believe he was transferred as an embryo into the womb of Queen Trishala by the commander of Indra's army. He attained kevalajnana (omniscience) at age 72, and his teachings were later organized into twelve Angas and fourteen Purvas at the Council of Pataliputra.

clues.clue: Traditionally lived during the 6th century BCE and is regarded as the founder of the contemporary cosmic cycle of Jainism
examples: This is the last Tirthankara. He lived during the 6th century BCE and is the founder of the contemporary cycle of Jainism.

clues.clue: As an embryo, was transferred into the womb of Queen Trishala by the commander of Indra's army — a belief held by Svetambaras
examples: As an embryo, the founder of this religion was believed to have been transferred into the womb of Trishala by the commander of Indra's army.

clues.clue: Son of King Siddhartha of the Ikshvaku dynasty's Jnatr/Gyat clan; some traditions hold he was a prince of Kosala
examples: This religion's founder was the son of Siddhartha, king of the Ikshvaku dynasty's Gyat clan.
thought to have been a prince of Kosala

clues.clue: Attained kevalajnana (omniscience) at age 72, becoming the 24th "fordmaker"
examples: At the age of 72, this figure attained kevalajnana under the asalah tree as the 24th "fordmaker."

clues.clue: His teachings were divided into twelve Angas and fourteen Purvas at the Council of Pataliputra after centuries of oral transmission
examples: The teachings of one leader of this faith were divided into twelve Angas and fourteen Purvas at the council of Patliputra after centuries of oral tradition.

clues.clue: Traditionally married to Yashoda, with whom he had a daughter
examples: According to some traditions he is married to Yashoda, with whom he had a daughter.

clues.clue: A temple at Pavapuri contains footmarks said to be his
examples: A temple of this religion at Pavapuri contains footmarks said to be from a figure important to this religion.

works.name: Tirthankaras
works.description: The class of 24 revered teacher-figures whose name means "ford-makers," for having created a passage across samsara toward moksha. Only the last two, Parshva and Mahavira, are considered historically attested. Images of tirthankaras generally lack distinctive features and are instead identified by lanchhana (symbols). Indra presides over the "five auspicious events" (Panch Kalyanaka) of each tirthankara's life, including birth and renunciation.

clues.clue: Name means "ford-makers" for creating a passage across samsara; only the last two (Parshva and Mahavira) are historically attested
examples: Name these venerated figures... Their name literally means 'ford-makers'
of which two are historically attested - these are the 'ford-makers'

clues.clue: Their lives include the "five auspicious events," or Panch Kalyanaka, including renunciation and the attainment of absolute knowledge
examples: The "five auspicious events" or Panch Kalyanaka in the lives of these figures include renunciation and absolute knowledge.

clues.clue: The god Indra presides over these events and appears as a white elephant in the first auspicious dream announcing one's conception
examples: This figure is represented in the first auspicious dream as a white elephant, signifying his role as a guardian deity... he serves as the teachers of the Tirthankaras.

clues.clue: Because their images otherwise lack distinctive features, they are identified by lanchhana (individual symbols)
examples: Images of these figures generally lack distinctive features and are identified by their lanchhana symbols, with the exception of one usually depicted with a snake crown.

clues.clue: The devotional Logassa Sutra praises the most recent 24 of them
examples: adherents also praise the most recent 24 tirthankaras in the Logassa Sutra.

works.name: Rishabha
works.description: The first of the 24 tirthankaras, legendarily said to have lived for roughly 600 quintillion years. Also known as Rishabhanatha, Rishabhadeva, or Adinatha. Father of Bahubali and Bharata; the pilgrimage site Palitana is dedicated to him.

clues.clue: First of the 24 tirthankaras, legendarily said to have lived for roughly 600 quintillion years
examples: The first of these figures, Rishabhanatha, is said to have lived for nearly 600 quintillion years.

clues.clue: Attained kevala jnana while meditating under a banyan tree
examples: While meditating under a banyan tree, Lord Rishabha, the first of these holy figures, attained a form of absolute knowledge known as Kevala jnana.

clues.clue: Father of Bahubali and Bharata; the pilgrimage site Palitana is dedicated to him, whom he visited 99 times in life
examples: One of Jainism's five most sacred tirthas is Palitana, which is dedicated to this first tirthankara. This father of Bahubali and Bharata visited Palitana 99 times during his life.

clues.clue: Only Svetambara Jains undertake the varshitapa fast, on alternating days, in his honor; he broke his own fast by accepting sugarcane juice from his great-grandson King Sreyamsa
examples: Only Svetambara Jains undertake the varshitapa fast on alternating days in honor of this first tirthankara, who broke his own fast by accepting sugarcane juice from his great-grandson King Sreyamsa.

works.name: Parshvanatha
works.description: The 23rd and penultimate tirthankara, immediately preceding Mahavira. Along with Mahavira, one of the only two tirthankaras considered historically attested.

clues.clue: 23rd and penultimate (second-to-last) tirthankara
examples: Parshvanatha was the 23rd and penultimate tirthankara, or "ford-maker."
the last two of which were Parshva and Mahavira

clues.clue: According to Svetambara tradition, founded the doctrine of "fourfold restraint" and was the only tirthankara to gain the title Kalikalkalpataru
examples: According to the Svetambara tradition, this tirthankara founded the doctrine of fourfold restraint. This tirthankara was the only one to gain the title of Kalikalkalpataru.

works.name: Mallinatha
works.description: The 19th tirthankara, known as the "lord of jasmine." The largest schism in Jainism partly stems from a dispute over Mallinatha's gender: Svetambaras hold Mallinatha was the sole female tirthankara and that women can attain moksha, while Digambaras hold all 24 tirthankaras (including Mallinatha) were male and that women cannot attain liberation without first being reborn as men.

clues.clue: Svetambaras believe this 19th tirthankara, the "lord of jasmine," was female; Digambaras believe all tirthankaras were male
examples: The Svetambaras believe this other Tirthankara, known as the 'lord of jasmine,' is a woman, which is in contrast with the Digambaras who believe that all the Tirthankaras were men.

clues.clue: The dispute over this tirthankara's gender underlies the largest schism in Jainism, over whether women can attain liberation without being reborn as men
examples: The Digambara and Svetambara sects of Jainism divided on whether women can achieve liberation without being reincarnated as male, which rests on whether this nineteenth tirthankara was male or female.
these two groups of Jains... disagree over the gender of the 19th tirthankara Mallinatha

works.name: Chandragupta Maurya
works.description: Legendary Mauryan emperor revered in Jain tradition for abdicating his throne. According to legend, his teacher Bhadrabahu convinced him that his conquests would cause a twelve-year famine, leading him to become a Jain monk and abdicate to Bindusara. He is said to have starved himself to death (sallekhana) in a cave in Karnataka, following the Digambara tradition.

clues.clue: His teacher Bhadrabahu convinced him his conquests would cause a twelve-year famine, leading him to abdicate his throne to Bindusara and become a Jain
examples: Many of these people allegedly moved south after one of these people convinced a ruler that his conquests would cause a twelve-year famine.
Legendarily, Bhadrabahu's teachings led a king to become one of these people and leave his kingdom to Bindusara.

clues.clue: Allegedly starved himself to death in a cave in Karnataka, following the Digambara tradition
examples: Chandragupta Maurya allegedly starved himself in accordance with the principles of what religious group, following the tradition of their Digambara school?
this figure starved himself to death in a cave in Karnataka

clues.clue: Svetambaras and Digambaras disagree over whether the original agamas were completely lost in a famine following his conquests
examples: Svetambara and Digambara Jains disagree over whether the original agamas were completely lost in a famine after this figure's conquests.

works.name: Digambara
works.description: The "sky-clad" sect of Jainism, whose ascetics renounce all possessions, including clothing, and go naked. Holds that women cannot attain moksha without first being reborn male, and that all 24 tirthankaras were men. Its central pilgrimage site, Shravanabelagola, contains the Gommateshwara statue.

clues.clue: "Sky-clad" sect whose ascetics go naked, keeping only a water gourd and a peacock-feather broom or whisk to sweep away insects
examples: Monks from this sect renounce almost all their possessions, only keeping a pot to boil water and a whisk broom to sweep away insects.
Ascetics of this sect carry a water gourd and a peacock-feather broom to sweep insects away, and nothing else.

clues.clue: Holds that women cannot attain moksha without first being reborn male, and that all 24 tirthankaras, including Mallinatha, were male
examples: Followers of this sect believe that Mallinatha was male since they teach that no woman can attain moksha.
This sect believes that Mallinath must not have been a woman, since women must be reborn as men to achieve enlightenment or become tirthankaras.

clues.clue: Believes its religion's sacred texts (the Agamas) have gradually disappeared, so the surviving Angas are not authentic
examples: This sect believes that all of its religion's sacred texts have gradually disappeared, such that existing anga are not authentic.

clues.clue: Central pilgrimage site Shravanabelagola contains the 57-foot Gommateshwara statue, ritually bathed in milk every 12 years
examples: A town central to this sect contains the 57-foot-tall Gommeteshwara statue, which is bathed in milk during a festival that takes place every 12 years.

clues.clue: The Adipurana, an epic by a member of this sect, recounts the life of Bahubali, credited as the first to achieve enlightenment in the present age
examples: The Adipurana, an epic written by a member of this sect, describes the life of Bahubali, whom this sect credits as being the first in the present age to achieve enlightenment.

clues.clue: According to its own tradition, founded by the 1st-century monk Sivabhuti after a dispute over whether a monk could keep a gifted shawl
examples: Name this sect supposedly founded by the 1st-century monk Sivabhuti following an argument over whether it was acceptable for a monk to keep a gifted shawl.

clues.clue: A 20th-century revival movement founded by Kanji Swami distinguished the "true pure knowledge self" from "the other" as a path to enlightenment
examples: A revival movement within this sect that advocates distinguishing between the 'true pure knowledge self' and 'the other' as a path to enlightenment was founded by Kanji Swami.

clues.clue: Philosophers Kundakunda and Pujyapada, of this sect, developed theories of the "pure soul" and wrote a major commentary on the Tattvartha Sutra
examples: Two members of this sect, Kundakunda and Pujyapada, respectively developed theories of the 'pure soul' and authored a major commentary on the Tattvarthasutra.

clues.clue: Revived the practice of sallekhana (fasting to death) in the 20th century
examples: Sallekhana, or fasting until death, was revived in the 20th century by monks of this Jain sect.

works.name: Svetambara
works.description: The "white-clad" sect of Jainism, whose monks wear white robes rather than going naked like Digambara ascetics. Holds that Mallinatha, the 19th tirthankara, was female and that women can attain moksha.

clues.clue: "White-clad" sect whose monks wear white robes, contrasted with naked Digambara monks
examples: in contrast to those of the Shvetambara, or white-clad, sect.
by contrast to the white robes of Svetambara monks.

clues.clue: Believes Mahavira's embryo was transferred into Trishala's womb by the commander of Indra's army
examples: The story of Mahavira's transfer to Trishala's womb as an embryo is a belief held by this 'white clad' sect of Jainism.

clues.clue: Undertake the varshitapa fast on alternating days in honor of Rishabha, the first tirthankara
examples: Only Svetambara Jains undertake the varshitapa fast on alternating days in honor of this first tirthankara.

clues.clue: Temples often include brightly painted statues of tirthankaras, unlike largely unpainted Digambara temples
examples: This religion's temples are largely unpainted among the Digambara sect, though the Svetambara sect often includes brightly-painted statues of great teachers called tirthankaras in its temples.

clues.clue: Recognizes five categories of false belief (mithyatva), while Digambaras recognize seven
examples: Svetambara Jains recognize this number of false beliefs under Mithyatva, while Digambara Jains recognize seven.

works.name: Sthanakavasi and Terapanth
works.description: Iconoclastic reform movements within Svetambara Jainism: Sthanakavasi arose in the 15th century, and Terapanth split from it in the 18th century. Both reject the worship of murtis (temple statues), unlike the majority "murti-pujaka" Jains.

clues.clue: Sthanakavasi is a 15th-century iconoclastic reform movement; Terapanth is its 18th-century offshoot
examples: This religion, which faced schisms from the iconoclastic 15th-century Sthanakavasi movement and its 18th-century Terapanth offshoot, had its first major split with the emergence of Digambara.

clues.clue: Both refuse to worship murtis (temple statues); most other Jains, who continue such rituals, are called "murti-pujaka"
examples: Sthanakavasi Jains and Terapanth Jains both refuse to worship these statues. The majority of Jains are called '[this term]-pujaka' because they continue performing rituals to these statues.

clues.clue: Acharya Tulsi founded the Anuvrat movement within Terapanth to universalize the religion's lesser vows, after leading a peace campaign during World War II
examples: An acharya named Tulsi founded the Anuvrat movement to universalize this religion's lesser vows after he led a peace campaign during World War II.

works.name: Ahimsa
works.description: The vow and principle of strict nonviolence toward all living creatures, the foremost of the Five Great Vows and the practice most identified with Jainism as a whole. Extended even to insects and single-sensed beings such as plants and water.

clues.clue: Central vow of nonviolence extended to all living creatures, including insects; the foremost of the Five Great Vows
examples: name this Indian minority religion, whose followers practise ahimsa, or nonviolence.
It is the foremost of the Five Great Vows.

clues.clue: Monks sweep the ground in front of them with a broom (such as the rajoharana or a peacock-feather whisk) to avoid stepping on and killing insects
examples: whose adherents sometimes sweep the ground in front of them as they walk in order to avoid killing insects
sweep in front of their feet to avoid harming small creatures in accordance with the doctrine of ahimsa.

clues.clue: Monks may wear cloth masks over their mouths to avoid inhaling and harming small creatures
examples: Members of this faith often wear masks over their mouths... to avoid harming small creatures.

clues.clue: Leads many adherents to practice vegetarianism
examples: Its vow of ahimsa, or strict non-violence, leads many adherents to vegetarianism.

clues.clue: Symbolized by an open hand (hamsa) with a dharmachakra wheel inside it
examples: a wheel called the dharmachakra appears inside one of these things symbolizing ahimsa.

works.name: Sallekhana
works.description: A ritual fast to death, undertaken voluntarily, typically near the end of life; a supplementary vow beyond the five main vows. Practitioners and Indian courts maintain it is not suicide. Its most extreme form, Padapopagamana, requires the practitioner to stand motionless "like a tree" until death.

clues.clue: Ritual fasting to death, undertaken voluntarily; in 2015 the Indian Supreme Court overturned a Rajasthan ban after ruling it is not suicide
examples: Practitioners of this religion often fast to death in a practice which is called sallekhana, which is not considered suicide.
In 2015, the Indian Supreme Court overturned a ban on a form of this practice which had been instituted in Rajasthan, accepting the argument made by its practitioners that it was not suicide.

clues.clue: A supplementary/optional vow taken in addition to the five main vows
examples: Sallekhana is considered a supplementary vow, taken in addition to the 5 main vows of this religion.

clues.clue: Its most extreme form, Padapopagamana, requires the practitioner to stand motionless "like a tree" until death
examples: which in its Padapopagamana form requires those who undertake it to stand 'like a tree' until it is over.

clues.clue: A less intensive form of this fast is practiced in the lead-up to the holiday of Paryushana
examples: Practitioners also engage in a less intensive variety of this practice in the leadup to Paryushana.

works.name: Samayika and Pratikraman
works.description: Two of the six daily obligatory rites (avashyaks). Samayika is a meditative practice of stillness aimed at equanimity, typically lasting 48 minutes for laypeople. Pratikraman, often performed afterward, is a ritual of asking forgiveness for one's sins, said as often as twice daily or as rarely as once yearly during Paryushana.

clues.clue: Samayika: a meditative practice of stillness aimed at equanimity, typically lasting 48 minutes for laypeople
examples: This principal Jain meditative practice typically lasts for 48 minutes when done by laypeople, and emphasizes being still in order to achieve equanimity.

clues.clue: Pratikraman, often performed after samayika, is a ritual of asking forgiveness ("micchami dukkadam"), repeated as often as twice a day or as rarely as once a year during Paryushana
examples: People repent for their sins twice a day in this religion's practice of pratikraman.
this ritual of asking for forgiveness, which Jains may repeat as often as twice a day or as little as once a year, during Paryushan. Jains say 'micchami dukkadam' to ask forgiveness.

clues.clue: The Namokar Mantra is often recited during samayika
examples: Samayika often involves reciting the Namokar mantra.

works.name: Anekantavada and Syadvada
works.description: The Jain epistemological principle that truth is "many-sided" (anekantavada) and no single viewpoint captures it completely, illustrated by a parable of blind men who each perceive an elephant differently. The related logical system syadvada uses the qualifier "syad" ("maybe," or "in some way") to develop a sevenfold scheme of predication.

clues.clue: Anekantavada ("many-sidedness") holds that truth is multi-faceted; illustrated by a parable of blind men who each perceive an elephant differently
examples: This religion regards truth as 'many-sided'
a parable about this animal illustrates the principle of anekantavada, or multiple correct viewpoints. Different blind men perceive it as a pillar, a hand fan, or a throne.

clues.clue: Logicians use the qualifier "syad" ("maybe") to develop a sevenfold/seven-valued logical scheme, e.g. "in some ways, it is, it is not, and it is indescribable"
examples: whose logicians advised using the word 'syad' to mean 'maybe' and created a seven-valued logic.
Thinkers of this religion developed a logic based on statements like 'in some ways, it is, it is not, and it is indescribable,' organized in a sevenfold scheme.

clues.clue: This epistemology was shared with the atomist Vaisheshika and Ajivika schools; Umasvati was a major scholar of it
examples: The Vaisheshikas and Ajivikas shared their atomism with this other Indian tradition, whose epistemology emphasized anekantavada. Umasvati was a major scholar of this school.

works.name: Karma, Jiva, and the Nine Tattvas
works.description: Jain metaphysics treats karma as physical matter that accumulates on the soul (jiva) through bodily activity. The soul is one of nine fundamental principles (navatattva), alongside non-living matter (ajiva). Souls are classified into five types by how many senses their possessing organism has.

clues.clue: Karma is conceived as physical particles that "influx" (asrava) onto the soul through bodily activity and are then "bound" (bandha) to it
examples: This religion holds that bodily activity causes the soul to receive an 'influx' of particles, which are then 'bound' to it, as two of its seven ontological principles.

clues.clue: Metaphysics recognizes nine fundamental principles (navatattva), including jiva (soul/living matter) and ajiva (non-living matter)
examples: Jain metaphysics recognizes nine of these fundamental principles, including jiva, which refers to the souls of living beings, and ajiva, which refers to non-living things.

clues.clue: The soul (jiva) is classified into five types by how many senses its possessing organism has, from one-sensed beings like plants and water up to five-sensed beings
examples: This religion sorts all living things into five tiers, according to how many senses they possess.
Jains group plants among the ekendriya, an embodied, one-sensed class of these beings.

clues.clue: Bad behavior causes particle accumulation on the soul unless one becomes a kevalin (an omniscient, liberated being)
examples: Adherents of this faith believe that as a person behaves badly, particles accumulate on their soul, unless they become a kevalin.

clues.clue: Four kasaya ("faults") — anger, pride, deceit (maya), and greed — impede reaching right belief (samyaktva), alongside mithyatva and nidana
examples: Maya, as referring to deceit, is considered as one of four Kasaya or faults in the philosophy of this Indian religion, along with anger, pride, and greed.

works.name: The Five Vows
works.description: The Five Great Vows (Mahavratas) — ahimsa (nonviolence), satya (truth), asteya (non-stealing), brahmacharya (celibacy), and aparigraha (non-possession) — are taken in full by monastics, and in reduced form (Anuvratas) by laypeople. Following these vows as "right conduct," alongside right knowledge and right faith, forms the "Three Jewels" (Ratnatraya) path to moksha.

clues.clue: The Five Great Vows (Mahavratas) are ahimsa, satya (truth), asteya (non-stealing), brahmacharya (celibacy/chastity), and aparigraha (non-possession)
examples: lists not stealing, celibacy, and non-possessiveness among its five vows.
which include a vow of truth called satya and a vow of sexual restraint called brahmacharya

clues.clue: Asteya ("non-stealing") is violated by acts like using short weights/measures or intentionally buying something below a fair price
examples: Violations of the vow described by this Sanskrit term include using short weights or measures and intentionally buying something at too low a price.

clues.clue: Laypeople take reduced vows called Anuvratas ("small vows"), alongside further categories called Gunavratas and Siksavratas
examples: Lay members of this faith follow twelve vows, divided into the Anuvratas, Gunavratas, and Siksavratas.
Name this religion whose lay members take five compulsory vows called vratas

clues.clue: Right conduct (following the vows), right knowledge, and right faith together form the "Three Jewels" (Ratnatraya) path to moksha
examples: In this religion, only the three dogmas of right knowledge, right faith, and right conduct lead to moksha.
which, along with right faith and right knowledge, make up this group of ways to attain moksha.

works.name: Jain Cosmology
works.description: An elaborate cosmological system describing cyclical time, symbolic geography, and other realms of being. Time is divided into six recurring periods; the distant past is said to have included "wishing trees" that granted requests, and Mount Meru sits at the center of the universe.

clues.clue: Time is divided into six recurring periods (aras); the current period is duhsama, during which human height and lifespan progressively shrink
examples: Jains believe that time is split into six time periods known by this name, with the current one being called duhsama.

clues.clue: The distant past is said to have included "wishing trees" (Kalpavriksha) that fulfilled needs when asked
examples: According to this non-Abrahamic faith, the distant past included 'wishing trees,' which fulfilled needs when asked.
a divine object of this type, granted her a daughter... What kind of object is the Kalpavriksha?

clues.clue: Mount Meru is the center of the universe in Jain (and Buddhist) cosmology; Indra carries newborn tirthankaras there
examples: When the next Tirthankara is born, Indra will take them to this location, the center of the universe in Buddhist and Jain cosmology.

clues.clue: The building Jambudweep models the terrestrial world as described in this cosmology
examples: One site in this religion is a building which models the terrestrial world as described in this religion's cosmology, and is called Jambudweep.

clues.clue: Nigodas are beings possessing only the sense of touch
examples: This faith's cosmology postulates the existence of beings with only the sense of touch called nigodas.

clues.clue: Pilgrimage sites called tirthas ("fords") are physical locations where tirthankaras achieved moksha; devotees may substitute study of tirth pat maps for physical pilgrimage
examples: Physical locations called by this name refer to holy places where 'makers' of them achieved moksha.
pilgrimages to tirthas can be replaced with studying tirth pat maps.

works.name: Agamas
works.description: The canonical scripture corpus, the orally-transmitted teachings of Mahavira. Digambaras and Svetambaras dispute whether the original Agamas were completely lost following a famine after Chandragupta Maurya's conquests, with surviving texts (Angas) regarded by Digambaras as inauthentic.

clues.clue: The orally-transmitted teachings of Mahavira, called agamas — a term also used by various Tantric traditions
examples: Though various Tantric traditions also have agamas, this religion uses the term to denote orally-transmitted teachings of its founder Mahavira.

clues.clue: Digambaras and Svetambaras dispute whether the original agamas were completely lost in a famine following Chandragupta Maurya's conquests
examples: Svetambara and Digambara Jains disagree over whether the original agamas were completely lost in a famine after this figure's conquests.
a great famine broke the transmission of all authentic Agama scriptures

clues.clue: The Upasaka Dashanga Sutra, a Svetambara agama, governs the laity and calls them sravaka/sravika
examples: The Upasaka Dashanga Sutra, a Svetambara agama, governs the Jain laity and calls them by [emphasize] this term.

works.name: Tattvartha Sutra
works.description: A foundational scripture written by Umasvati (Umaswati), the only text considered authoritative by both the Digambara and Svetambara sects. Contains the aphorism "souls render service to one another," using the term jiva for living matter.

clues.clue: Written by Umasvati/Umaswati; the only text considered authoritative by both Digambara and Svetambara Jains
examples: This scripture by Umaswati is the only text authoritative to both Digambara and Svetambara Jains.

clues.clue: Contains the aphorism "souls render service to one another," using the term jiva for living matter
examples: A motto of this religion, 'Souls render service to one another,' makes use of its term for 'living matter,' or jiva.
It contains the Jain aphorism 'Souls render service to one another.'

works.name: Namokar Mantra
works.description: Also called the Navakara Mantra, the most important Jain prayer, recited by adherents while bowing ("namo") to the Panch Parameshthi ("Supreme Five"), including tirthankaras, other arihants, and siddhas.

clues.clue: Most important Jain prayer, recited while bowing to the Panch Parameshthi ("Supreme Five"), which includes arihants and siddhas
examples: Adherents of this religion venerate the pancha-parameshthi, including siddhas and arihants, in the Navakara Mantra, this religion's most important prayer.

clues.clue: Opens by stating "I bow to [these people]" — bowing to arihants, of whom tirthankaras are a subgroup, distinguished from the less accessible siddhas
examples: Samayika often involves reciting the Namokar mantra, which begins by stating 'I bow to [these people].' Tirthankaras are a subgroup of these people, who become the less accessible siddhas.

clues.clue: The word "namo" in it refers to the act of bowing/prostration, also performed while reciting the Khamasamana Sutra
examples: The word namo in the Navakara Mantra refers to this action, which Jains perform while reciting the Khamasamana Sutra.

works.name: Paryushana
works.description: A major annual Jain holiday centered on repentance and intensified fasting, during which the pratikraman ritual of asking forgiveness is performed.

clues.clue: A major annual holiday preceded by intensified fasting practice
examples: Practitioners also engage in a less intensive variety of this practice in the leadup to Paryushana.
Paryushana is a major holiday for this religion.

clues.clue: The pratikraman ritual of asking forgiveness ("micchami dukkadam") is performed during this holiday, as often as twice daily or as rarely as once yearly
examples: which Jains may repeat as often as twice a day or as little as once a year, during Paryushan.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: Standard closing/identifying clues for Jainism as a whole, plus miscellaneous facts about its adherents, symbols, and history that did not cluster into a larger section.

clues.clue: Indian minority religion whose adherents practice ahimsa, or nonviolence
examples: name this Indian minority religion, whose followers practise ahimsa, or nonviolence.
identify this religion whose adherents practice ahimsa, or nonviolence, and was founded by Mahavira.

clues.clue: Its name derives from a Sanskrit word meaning "to conquer," reflecting its focus on self-control
examples: Its name is derived from a word meaning 'to conquer', as its main focus is self-control.

clues.clue: One of the Sramana movements (alongside Buddhism), which rejected Vedic tradition
examples: originally one of the Sramana movements along with Buddhism.
Name this sramana religion whose adherents follow perspectivism and asceticism

clues.clue: The ultimate goal is moksha — emancipation from the cycle of reincarnation (samsara)
examples: the ultimate goal of followers of this religion is emancipation from reincarnation, or moksha.

clues.clue: In 1974, adopted an official emblem with three dots near the top and a dot inside a curve, representing a liberated/enlightened soul
examples: In 1974, this faith adopted an official emblem which contains three dots near the top and an additional dot inside a curve, representing an enlightened soul.

clues.clue: Wealthy Jain merchant families such as Shantidas Jhaveri and the Jagat Seth family served the Mughals and the Nawabs of Bengal
examples: Extraordinarily rich examples of these people include Shantidas Jhaveri and the Jagat Seth family, who served the Mughals and the Nawabs of Bengal.

clues.clue: A Gujarati Jain group that migrated from Palanpur came to control Antwerp's diamond trade, previously run by the Hasidic community
examples: A Gujarati group of these people who migrated from Palanpur usurped Antwerp's Hasidic community's control of the diamond trade.

clues.clue: In ancient South Asian sources, adherents were referred to by a term meaning "free from knots"
examples: In ancient South Asian sources, this kind of people were referred to by a term referring to their status as being 'free from knots.'

clues.clue: The earliest known Jain image, held at the Patna Museum, dates to approximately the 3rd century BCE
examples: The earliest known image made by members of this religion is held at the Patna museum and is dated to approximately the 3rd century BC.

clues.clue: Stone-carved temples at Mount Abu are a prominent pilgrimage site
examples: A prominent site of pilgrimage for this religion is a series of stone-carved temples at Mount Abu.

clues.clue: Historical patrons included the Tulu queen Abbakka Chowta and the Rashtrakuta king Krishna I, whose dynasty carved Jain monuments in the Ellora Caves
examples: The Tulu rani Abbakka Chowta, who defended her kingdom against the Portuguese, was notable for patronizing this religion.
Several Rashtrakuta carvings in the Ellora Caves reflect King Krishna I's patronage of this tradition.

clues.clue: Medieval stories from this faith were collected in The Forest of Thieves and the Magic Garden by Yale scholar Phyllis Granoff
examples: Medieval stories from this faith were collected in The Forest of Thieves and the Magic Garden by Yale scholar Phyllis Granoff.

comprehensive_summary: Jainism is an ancient Indian minority religion, one of the Sramana movements alongside Buddhism, whose defining principle is ahimsa — strict nonviolence extended to all living beings, including insects, which monks avoid harming by sweeping their path and sometimes wearing mouth-masks. Adherents venerate 24 tirthankaras ("ford-makers"), teacher-figures who cross samsara to help others reach moksha; only the last two, Parshvanatha (23rd) and Mahavira (24th), are considered historically attested. Mahavira, traditionally dated to the 6th century BCE, is regarded as the founder of the religion's current cosmic cycle; Svetambara tradition holds he was transferred as an embryo into Queen Trishala's womb, and he is said to have attained omniscience (kevalajnana) at 72. Jainism is divided into two principal sects: Digambara ("sky-clad"), whose ascetics renounce clothing entirely, and Svetambara ("white-clad"), whose monks wear white robes. Their largest schism partly concerns the gender of the 19th tirthankara, Mallinatha — Svetambaras hold Mallinatha was female and that women can attain moksha, while Digambaras hold all tirthankaras were male and that women must be reborn male first. Further reform movements, the iconoclastic Sthanakavasi and its offshoot Terapanth, reject temple-statue worship altogether. Distinctive Jain philosophy includes anekantavada ("many-sidedness," illustrated by the parable of blind men and an elephant) and its sevenfold syadvada logic, which qualifies truth-claims with the word "syad" ("maybe"). Jain metaphysics holds that karma is physical matter which "influxes" onto the soul (jiva) through action and becomes "bound" to it; the soul is one of nine fundamental categories (navatattva) and is classified into five types by sense-count. Lay and monastic life is structured around the Five Great Vows (Mahavratas) — ahimsa, satya (truth), asteya (non-stealing), brahmacharya (celibacy), and aparigraha (non-possession) — whose observance, alongside right knowledge and right faith, forms the "Three Jewels" path to moksha. Distinctive practices include sallekhana, a ritual fast to death considered neither suicide nor prohibited by Indian courts; the daily rites of samayika (meditation) and pratikraman (ritual forgiveness); and recitation of the Namokar Mantra, the religion's central prayer. The major holiday Paryushana centers on fasting and repentance. Canonical scripture, the orally-transmitted Agamas, is disputed between sects — Digambaras hold the originals were lost in a famine following Chandragupta Maurya's legendary conquests and subsequent self-starvation — while the Tattvartha Sutra by Umasvati is the sole text both sects accept as authoritative.
recursive_suggestions: Mahavira — could deserve his own deep dive as the 24th tirthankara and traditional founder; watch for overlap with this page's Figure section
Digambara vs. Svetambara schism — the Mallinatha gender dispute and clothing dispute are dense enough to be their own cross-linked topic if a future set clues them heavily
Anekantavada / syadvada — the sevenfold logic scheme has appeared in Philosophy bonuses (e.g. alongside Nagarjuna's catuskoti) as well as Religion ones; flag for Philosophy category cross-reference
Chandragupta Maurya — recurring figure in South Asian History topics; his Jain conversion/sallekhana legend should cross-link if a History topic exists for him
Buddhism — frequently contrasted with Jainism as a fellow Sramana movement; useful cross-reference once a Buddhism topic is processed
